# Page 510

494 Phosphorus

Ch. 12

Phosphine and its Derivatives as Ligands:75-7)

A wide variety of 3-coordinate phosphorus(III) compounds are known and these have been extensively stadied as ligands
because of their significance in improving our understanding of the stability and reactivity of many coordination complexes.
Among the most studied of these ligands are PH3, PF3 (p. 495), PCls (p. 496), PR3 (R = alkyl), PPhs and P(OR)3, together
with a large number of “mixed” ligands such as Me2NPF2, PMePhg, etc., and many muitidentate (chelating) ligands such
as PhgPCH)CH2PPhy, etc.

In many of their complexes PF3 and PPh3 (for example) resemble CO (p. 926) and this at one time encouraged
the belief that their bonding capabilities were influenced not only by the factors (p. 198) which affect the stability
of the ¢ P->M inleraction which uses the one-pair of electrons on P!! and a vacant orbital on M, but also by the
possibility of synergic  back-donation from a “nonbonding” dj pair of electrons on the metal into a “vacant” 3d,
orbital on P, It is, however, not clear to what extent, if any, the o and 7 bonds reinforce each other, and more recent
descriptions are based on an MO approach which uses all (o and zr) orbitals of appropriate symmetry on both the phos-
phine and the metal-containing moiety. To the extent that o and 2 bonding effects on the stability of metal-phosphorus
bonds can be isolated from each other and from steric factors (see below) the accepted sequence of effects is as fol-
lows:

 

@ bonding: PBu' > P(OR); > PR3 © PPh3 > PH3 > PF; > P(OPh);
x bonding: PF; > P(OPh); > PH3 > P(OR); > PPhy © PR3 > PBu,
Steric interference: PBu’ > PPh3 > P(OPh); > PMe3 > P(OR)3 > PF; > PH3

 

Steric factors are frequently dominant, particularly with bulky ligands, and their influence on the course of many reactions
is crucial, One measure of the “size” of a ligand in so far as it affects bond formation is C. A. Toiman’s cone angie
(1970) which is the angie at the metal atom of the cone swept out by the van der Waals radii of the groups attached to
P. This will, of course, be dependent on the actual interatomic distance between M and P. For the particular case of Ni.
for which a standard value of 228 pm was adopted for Ni-P, the calculated values for the cone angle are:

 

 

Ligand PH3 PF3 (OMe); P(OEt); PMe3 P(OPh)3 PCis
Cone angle 87° 104° 107° 109° 118° 121° 125°
Ligand PEt; PPh3 PPh PBus P(o-tol)3 P(mesityl)3

Cone angle 132° 145° 160° 182° 195° 212°

 

Bulky tertiary phosphine ligands exert both steric and electronic influences when they form complexes (since an
increase in bulkiness of a substituent on P increases the inter-bond angles and this in turn can be thought of as an increase
in “p-character” of the ione-pair of electrons on P). For example, the sterically demanding di-t-butyiphosphines, PBu}R
(R = alkyl or aryl), promote spatially less-demanding features such as hydride formation, coordinative unsaturation at
the metal centre, and even the stablization of unusual oxidation states, such as Ir!!, They also favour internal C- or
O- metallation reactions for the same reasons. Indeed, the metallation of C-H and C-P bonds of coordinated tertiary
phosphines can be considered as examples of intramolecular oxidative addition, and these have important mechanistic
implications for homogeneous and heterogeneous catalysis.

‘Other notable examples are the orthometallation (orthophenylation) reactions of many complexes of aryl phosphines
(PAr3) and aryl phosphites P(OAr)3 with platinum metals in particular, e.g.:

hheat(—Ha)

 

[RuCIH(P(OPh)3}4] (PhO): PO-CéH,-RuCi{P(OPh)3]}3

 

Ho

See also C. A, MCAULIFFE (ed.), Transition-Metal Complexes
of Phosphorus, Arsenic and Antimony Donor Ligands, Mac-
millan, London, 1972.

(1977). An extensive review with over 1700 references
arranged by element and by technique but with no assessment
or generalizations.

§12.3.3

its hydrophosphorylation of formaldehyde in
aqueous hydrochloric acid solution:

PH; + 4HCHO + HCl —-> [P(CH,OH),]CI

The tetrakis(hydroxymethyl)phosphonium chlo-
ride so formed is the major ingredient with urea-
formaldehyde or melamine-formaldehyde resins
for the permanent flame-proofing of cotton cloth.

Of the many other hydrides of phosphorus,
diphosphane (diphosphine), P2H4, is the most
studied. It is best made’ by treating CaP with
cold oxygen-free water. Passage of PH; through
an electric discharge at 5-10kV is an alternative
method for small amounts. PH, is a colourless,
volatile liquid (mp —99°) which is thermally
unstable even below room temperature and is
decomposed slowly by water. Its vapour pressure
at 0°C is 70.2mmHg but partial decomposition
precludes precise determination of the bp (63.5°
extrap); d~1.014gcm~* at 20°C. Electron-
diffraction measurements on the gas establish
the gauche-Cz configuration (p. 428) with P—P
222pm, P-H 145 pm, and the angle H-P—H
91.3°, though vibration spectroscopy suggests a
trans-C2», configuration in the solid phase. These
results can be compared with those for the halides
P2X4 on p. 498.

The next member of the open-chain series
P,H,+2 is P3Hs, ie. PH2PHPH2, a colourless liq-
uid that can be stored in the dark at —-80° for
several days.'°7-7)) It can be made by dispropor-
tionation (2P;H; ——> P3Hs + PH3) but it is dif-
ficult to purify because of its own fairly ready dis-
proportionation and reactivity, e.g. 2P;Hs ——>
P4Hs + P2H4; and P3Hs + P2H, —> PyH, +
PH3. Tetraphosphane(6), PsHe, exists as an equi-
librium mixture of the two structural isomers
H2PPHPHPH2 () and P(PH2)3(i), and itself
reacts with P;Hs at —20° according to the ide-
alized stoichiometry P4Hs + P3Hs ——> 2PH3 +
PsHs, i.e. cyclo-(PH)s. All members of the series
cyclo-P,H, (n = 3-10) have been detected mass
spectrometrically in the thermolysis products
from P2H4.

Polycyclic polyphosphanes are often best pre-
pared by direct protonation of the correspond-
ing polyphosphide anions (Figs. 12.11 and 12.12)

Phosphorus halides 495

with HX, though other routes are also available.
Thus, treatment of P;*~ yields P7H?~, P7H)~
and P7Hy by sucessive protonation of the three
2-connected P~ sites. The alkyl derivatives are
more stable than the parent polycyclic phosphanes
and provide many examples of the elegant solu-
tion of complex conformational problems by the
use of nmr spectroscopy.{67-

12.3.3 Phosphorus halides

Phosphorus forms three series of halides P2X4,
PX; and PXs. All 12 compounds may exist,
although there is considerable doubt about PIs.
Numerous mixed halides PX2Y and PX2Y3 are
also known as well as various pseudohalides such
as P(CN)3, P(CNO);, P(CNS)3 and their mixed
halogeno-counterparts. The compounds form an
extremely useful extended series with which
to follow the effect of progressive substitution
on various properties, and the pentahalides are
particularly significant in spanning the “ionic-
covalent” border, so that they exist in various
structural forms depending on the nature of the
halogen, the phase of aggregation, or the polarity
of the solvent. Some subhalides such as PsX2
and P7X3, and some curious polyhalides such as
PBr; and PBrj; have also been characterized.
Physical properties of the binary halides are
summarized in Table 12.3 (on the next page).
Ternary (mixed) halides tend to have properties
intermediate between those of the parent binary
halides.

Phosphorus trihalides

All 4 trihalides are volatile reactive compounds
which feature pyramidal molecules. The fluoride
is best made by the action of CaF), ZnF)
or AsF; on PCl3, but the others are formed
by direct halogenation of the element. PF3 is
colourless, odourless and does not fume in air,
but is very hazardous due to the formation
of a complex with blood haemoglobin (cf.

80]. ToRNIEPORTH-OETTING and T. KLaP6rkE, J. Chem. Soc.
Chem. Commun., 132-3 (1990).

CO, p. 1101). It is about as toxic as COC].
The similarity of PF; and CO as ligands was
first noted by J. Chatt®!) and many complexes
with transition elements are now known,©?)
e.g. [Ni(CO),(PF3)4-n] (2 = 0-4), [Pd(PF3),],
[Pt(PF3)4], [CoH(PF3)s], [Co2(-PF2)2(PF3)6],
etc. Such complexes can be prepared by ligand
replacement reactions, by fluorination of PCl;
complexes, by direct reaction of PF; with metal
salts or even by direct reaction of PF; with metals
at elevated temperatures and pressures.

PF3, unlike the other trihalides of phosphorus,
hydrolyses only slowly with water, the products
being phosphorous acid and HF: PF; + 3H20 >
H3PO3 + 3HF.

The reaction is much more rapid in alkaline
solutions, and in dilute aqueous KHCO; solutions
the intermediate monofluorophosphorous acid is
formed:

2% aq KHCO,

PCI; is the most important compound of the
group and is made industrially on a large scale*
by direct chlorination of phosphorus suspended in
a precharge of PCl; — the reaction is carried out
under reflux with continuous take-off of the PCl;
formed. PCI; undergoes many substitution reac-
tions, as shown in the diagram, and is the main
source of organophosphorus compounds. Partic-
ularly notable are PR3, PR,Cl3-,, PRr(OR)3—n,
(PhO)3;PO, and (RO)3;PS. Many of these com-
pounds are made on the 1000-tonne scale pa, and
the major uses are as oil additives, plasticizers,
fiame retardants, fuel additives and intermediates
in the manufacture of insecticides.®) PC]; is also
readily oxidized to the important phosphorus(V)
derivatives PCl;, POC; and PSCl. It is oxi-
dized by As2O3 to POs though this is not the
commercial route to this compound (p. 505). It
fumes in moist air and is more readily hydrol-
ysed (and oxidized) by water than is PF3. With
cold N2O4 (—10°) it undergoes a curious oxida-
tive coupling reaction to give Cl;P=N-POCh,

* World production exceeds one third of a million tonnes
pa; of this USA produces ~155 000 tonnes, Western Europe
~115000 and Japan ~35 000 tonnes pa.

P2Cl,4 can be made (in low yield) by passing an
electric discharge through a mixture of PCI; and
Hp under reduced pressure or by microwave dis-
charge through PCI; at 1-5 mmHg pressure. The
compound decomposes slowly at room tempera-
ture to PCl; and an involatile solid, and can be
hydrolysed in basic solution to give an equimolar
mixture of P2H4 and P2(OH),.

Little is known of P2Brg, said to be produced
by an obscure reaction in the system C2H,-
PBr3—AlpBrg.°© By contrast, P21, is the most
stable and also the most readily made of the 4
tetrahalides; it is formed by direct reaction of
I, and red P at 180° or by Iz and white Py in
CS) solution, and can also be made by reducing
PI; with red P, or PCl; with iodine. Its X-ray
crystal structure shows that the molecules of P2I4
adopt the trans-, centrosymmetric (C2;,) form (see
N2Hq, p. 428, N2Fq, p. 439). Reaction of Polg
with sulfur in CS2 yields P2I,S2, which probably
has the symmetrical structure

but most reactions of P2I, result in cleavage
of the P-P bond, e.g. Br. gives PBrlz in 90%
yield. Hydrolysis yields various phosphines and
oxoacids of P, together with a small amount of
hypophosphoric acid, (HO)2(O)PP(O)(OH)2.
Several ternary diphosphorus  tetrahalides,
P)X, Yan, (X, Y=Cl, Br, I) have recently

products of the initial reactions included P7Br3
and P71; which are structurally related to P7H3
(p. 495). None of these novel subhalides has been
isolated pure.(*7)

Phosphorus pentahalides

Considerable theoretical and stereochemical
interest attaches to these compounds because
of the variety of structures they adopt; PCls is
also an important chemical intermediate. Thus,
PFs is molecular and stereochemically non-rigid
(see below), PCls is molecular in the gas phase,
ionic in the crystalline phase, [PCl4}*{PCl6]~,
and either molecular or ionically dissociated in
solution, depending on the nature of the solvent.
PBrs is also ionic in the solid state but exists
as [PBr4]*[Br] rather than [PBra]*[PBre}~. The
pentaiodide does not exist (except perhaps as
Pls.12, but certainly not as PlytI~ as originally
claimed®*)),

PFs is a thermally stable, chemically reac-
tive gas which can be made either by fluorinat-
ing PCls with AsF; (or CaF), or by thermal
decomposition of NaPFs, Ba(PF¢)2 or the cor-
responding diazonium salts. Single-crystal X-ray
analysis (at —164°C) indicates a trigonal bipyra-
midal structure with P—F,, (158.0pm) being

significantly longer than P-F,, (152.2pm).*)
This confirms the deductions from a gas phase

trum, as recorded down to —100°C, shows only

from “instantaneous” for electron diffraction) all

their positions more rapidly than this, a process
termed “pseudorotation” by R. S. Berry (1960);
indeed, PF; was the first compound to show
this effect. The proposed mechanism is illus-
trated in Fig. 12.13 and is discussed more fully in
ref. 91; the barrier to notation has been calculated
as 16 + 2kJ mol!)

although molecular monomers R4PF and an ionic
dimer {PR4]*[PR4F2}~ also exist.

liquid (or gas). Again, when dissolved in non-
polar solvents such as CCl, or benzene, PCls is
monomeric and molecular, whereas in ionizing
solvents such as MeCN, MeNO) and PhNO; there

are two competing ionizing equilibria:©

2PCls ==> [PCl;]* + [PCl6]~
PCls ===> [PCl4]* + Cl-

 

As might be expected, the former equilibrium
predominates at higher concentrations of PCls
(above about 0.03 mol I~!) whilst the latter pre-
dominates below this concentration. The P—Cl
distances (pm) in these various species are:
PCls 214 (axial), 202 (equatorial); [PCl4]* 197;
[PCle]~ 208 pm. Ionic isomerism is also known
and, in addition to [PCl4]*[PCle]~, another
(metastable) crystalline phase of constitution
(PCl4]}[PCle]-CI- can be formed either by
application of high pressure or by crystallizing
PCI; from solutions of dichloromethane contain-
ing Br2 or SCl.°”) When gaseous PCls (in equi-
librium with PCl; +Clz) is quenched to 15K
the trigonal-bipyramidal molecular structure is
retained; this forms an ordered molecular crys-
talline lattice on warming to ~130K, but fur-
ther warming towards room temperature results in
chloride-ion transfer to give [PCl4]* [PCl,]~.©®
The first alkali metal salt of [PCls]~, CsPClg, has
only recently been made.)

The delicate balance between ionic and
covalent forms is influenced not only by the state
of aggregation (solid, liquid, gas) or the nature of
the solvent, but also by the effect of substituents.
Thus PhPCl, is molecular with Ph equatorial
whereas the corresponding methyl derivative is
ionic, [MePCl3]* CI~. Despite this the [PhPCl3]*

PCls is made on an industrial scale by the
reaction of Cl) on PCI; dissolved in an equal vol-
ume of CCl4. World production probably exceeds
20000 tonnes pa. On the laboratory scale Cl,
gas (or liquid) can be passed directly into PCs.
PCls reacts violently with water to give HCI and
H3PO, but in equimolar amounts the reaction can
be moderated to give POCI;:

PCls + H2O —-+ POC] + 2HCI

PCls chlorinates alcohols to alkyl halides and
carboxylic acids to the corresponding RCOCI.
When heated with NH,Cl the phosphonitrilic
chlorides are obtained (p. 536). These and other
reactions are summarized in the diagram.)

The chlorination of phosphonic and phosphinic

thiocyanates are perhaps the best known. Most are
volatile liquids, e.g.

POBr; can be made by similar methods,
but POF; is usually made by fluorination
of POCI; using a metal fluoride (e.g. M=
Na, Mg, Zn, Pb, Ag, etc.). POI; was first
made in 1973 by iodinating POCI; with Lil,
or by reacting ROP with iodine (ROPI; +
Ip > RI + POIs). Mixed phosphoryl halides,
POX,, Y3_,, and pseudohalides (e.g. X = NCO,
NCS) are known, as also are the thiophosphoryl
halides PSX3, e.g.

P,Ss + 3PCl; ——> SPSCl;

CSp/dark

AICI;
PCI; +S ——> PSCh; Pl; +S ———> PS

Most of the phosphoryl and thiophosphoryl com-
pounds are colourless gases or volatile liquids
though PSBr; forms yellow crystals, mp 37.8°,
POI; is dark violet, mp 53°, and PSI; is red-
brown, mp 48°. All are monomeric tetrahedral
(C3,) or pseudotetrahedral. Some physical prop-
erties are in Table 12.4. The P—O interatomic

104 A.V. KirsaNov, ZH. K. GORBATENKO and N. G. FESH-
CHENKO, Pure Appl. Chem. 44, 125~39 (1975).

distance in these compounds generally falls in the
range 154-158 pm, the small value being con-
sistent with considerable “double-bond charac-
ter”. Likewise the P—S distance is relatively short
(185-194 pm).

The phosphoryl and thiophosphory! halides
are reactive compounds that hydrolyse readily
on contact with water. They form adducts with
Lewis acids and undergo a variety of substitution
reactions to form numerous organophosphorus
derivatives and phosphate esters. Thus, alcohols
give successively (RO)POCI, (RO)2POCI and
(RO);PO; phenols react similarly but more
slowly. Likewise, amines yield (RNH)POCI2,
(RNH):POCI and (RNH);PO whereas Grignard
reagents yield R,POCI_, (nm = 1-3). Many
of these compounds find extensive use as oil
additives, insecticides, plasticizers, surfactants or
flame retardants, and are manufactured on the
multikilotonne scale.

In addition to the monophosphorus phosphoryl
and thiophosphoryl compounds discussed above,
several poly-phosphoryl and -thiophosphoryl
halides have been characterized. Pyrophos-
phoryl fluoride, O=PF;—O—P(=O)F2 (mp
—0.1°, bp 72° extrap) and the white crys-
talline cyclic tetramer (O=PE)—Ole were

obtained by subjecting equimolar mixtures
of PF; and Q) to a silent electric dis-
charge at —70°. Pyrophosphoryl chloride,
O=PCl,—O—P(=O)Cly is conveniently pre-
pared by passing Cl into a boiling suspension

---
# Page 519

§12.3.5
of P,Ojo in PCI; diluted with CCl:
PsOio + 4PCh + 4Ch, ——> 2P203Cl, + 4POCI;

It is a colourless, odourless, non-fuming, oily
liquid, mp —16.5°, bp 215° (decomp), with
reactions similar to those of POCI;. Sealed-
tube reactions between P4O,9 and POCI3 at
200-—230° give more highly condensed cyclic and
open-chain polyphosphoryl chlorides. A rather
different structural motif occurs in P2S,4F4; this
compound is obtained by fluorinating P4Sio
with an alkali-metal fluoride to give the anion
[S2PF2]~ which is then oxidized by bromine to
P2S4F, (bp 60° at 10mmHg). Vibrational and
nmr spectra are consistent with the structure
F2(S)PSSP(S)F2.

Bromination of P,S7 in cold CS? yields,
in addition to PBr; and PSBr3, two further
thiobromides P2S¢Br2 (mp 118° decomp) and
P2SsBr4 (mp 90° decomp). The first of these
has the cyclic structure shown in which the
ring adopts a skew-boat configuration. An even
more complex, bicyclic arrangement is found in
the orange-yellow compound P4S3l, (mp 120°
decomp) which is formed (together with several
other products) when equiatomic amounts of P,
S and I are allowed to react. The P and S atoms
are arranged in two 5-membered rings having a
common P-S—P group as shown; in each there
is a P—P group and the I atoms are bonded in
cis-configuration to the P atoms not common to
the two rings. The orange compound P2S I, (mp
94°) was mentioned on p. 498.

Phosphorus oxides, sulfides, selenides and related compounds 503

By contrast to the plethora of simple oxo-
halides and thiohalides of PY, the corresponding
derivatives of P™ are fugitive species that require
matrix isolation techniques for preparation and
characterization: CIPO, BrPO, FPS and
BrPS all form non-linear triatomic molecules,
as expected. The corresponding oxosulfide,
BrP(O)S," and its thio-analogue, FP(S)S,"°””
have also recently been isolated.

12.3.5 Phosphorus oxides, sulfides,
selenides and related
compounds

The oxides and sulfides of phosphorus are
amongst the most important compounds of the
element. At least 6 binary oxides and 9 well-
defined sulfides are known, together with a
similar number of selenides and several oxo-
sulfides. It will be convenient to discuss first
the preparation and structure of each group of
compounds and then to mention the chemi-
cal reactions of the more important members
in so far as they are known. It is notable
that, in contrast to the ubiquitous NO and its
many complexes (pp. 445 ff), little is known
about its analogue, PO (see p. 506), although
it is probably the most abundant P-containing
molecule in interstellar clouds. The first com-
plex with a PO ligand was first synthesized
as recently as 1991, when dark green crys-
tals of the square-based pyramidal hetero-atom
cluster [W(CO)4{Ni(n°-CsHPr,)}2(12:77,7-P2)]
was oxidized with bis(trimethylsilyl) peroxide,
(Me3Si)202, to yield black crystals of the cor-
responding [W(CO),{Ni(q°-CsHPr4)}2(H:97s17-
PO),].(1°8)

Oxides

P4O¢ is obtained by controlled oxidation of Ps
in an atmosphere of 75% O) and 25% N2
at 90mmHg and ~50° followed by distillation
of the product from the mixture. Careful

tetrahedral symmetry and comprises 4 fused
6-membered P303 heterocycles each with the
chair conformation as shown in Fig, 12.14.11
When P4O¢ is heated to 200-400° in a
sealed, evacuated tube it disproportionates into
red phosphorus and a solid-solution series of

The a-phase has a composition in the range
P4Og.1~P4Op.2 and comprises a solid solution of
oxides in which one or two of the “external”
O atoms in PsOjo have been removed. The
B-phase has a composition range P4Ogo-P4O77

an oxidant) at room temperature. The molecular
structure and dimensions of P,Q7 are given
in Fig. 12.14 from which it is apparent that
there is a gradual lengthening of P—O distances
in the sequence PY—O, < PY —O, < P"'-O,.
Similar trends are apparent in the dimensions of
the other members of the series P,O¢;, shown

(122~132°), as expected.

P4O¢ hydrolyses in cold water to give
H3PO3 i.e. HP(O)(OH)2; this is interesting in
view of the structure of P,Q, and implies
an oxidative rearrangement of {P-OH} to
{H-P=O)} (p. 514). The oxide itself ignites
and burns when heated in air; the progress
of the reaction depends very much on the

purity of the oxide and the conditions employed,
and, when traces of elemental phosphorus
are present in the oxide, the reaction is

reacts readily (often violently) with many simple
inorganic and organic compounds but well-
characterized products have rarely been isolated
until recently. It behaves as a ligand and
successively displaces CO from [Ni(CO),] to
give compounds such as [P,O¢{Ni(CO)3}.],
(Ni(CO)2(P40¢)2]_ and [Ni(CO)(P,O¢)3]._ With
diborane adducts of formula [P40¢(BH3),.] (n =
1-3) are obtained.

“Phosphorus pentoxide”, P,OQjo, is the com-
monest and most important oxide of phosphorus.
It is formed as a fine white smoke or powder
when phosphorus burns in air and, when con-
densed rapidly from the vapour phase in this way,
is obtained in the H (hexagonal) form compris-
ing tetrahedral molecules as shown in Fig. 12.14.
This compound and the other phosphorus oxides
are the first we have considered that feature the
{PO,} group as a structural unit; this group domi-
nates most of phosphate chemistry and will recur
repeatedly during the rest of this chapter. The
common hexagonal form of P,Ojo is, in fact,
metastable and can be transformed into several
other modifications by suitable thermal or high-
pressure treatment. A metastable orthorhombic
(O) form is obtained by heating H for 2h at 400°
and the stable orthorhombic (O’) form is obtained
after 24h at 450°. Both consist of extensive
sheet polymers of interlocking heterocyclic rings
composed of fused {PO,} groups. There is also
a high-pressure form and a glass, which prob-
ably consists of an irregular three-dimensional
network of linked {PO,} tetrahedra. These poly-
meric forms are hard and brittle because of the
P-O-P bonds throughout the lattice and, as

expected, they are much less volatile and reac-
tive than the less-dense molecular H form. For
example, whilst the common H form hydrolyses
violently, almost explosively, with evolution of
much heat, the polymeric forms react only slowly
with water to give, finally, H3PO,. Some proper-
ties of the various polymorphs are compared in
Table 12.5. The limpid liquid obtained by rapidly
heating the H form contains P,O,9 molecules
but these rapidly polymerize and rearrange to
layer or three-dimensional polymeric forms with
a concomitant drop in the vapour pressure and an
increase in the viscosity and mp.

Because of its avidity for water, P4Ojo is
widely used as a dehydrating agent, but its
efficacy as a desiccant is greatly impaired by the
formation of a crusty surface film of hydrolysis
products unless it is finely dispersed on glass
wool. Its largest use is in the industrial production
of ortho- and poly-phosphoric acids (p. 520)
but it is also an intermediate in the production
of phosphate esters. Thus, triethylphosphate is
made by reacting P4Ojo with diethyl ether to
form ethylpolyphosphates which, on subsequent
pyrolysis and distillation, yield the required
product:

heat
P4019 + 6EtXO ——> 4PO(OEt);

Direct reaction with alcohols gives mixed mono-
and di-alky! phosphoric acids by cleavage of the
P-O-P bonds:

65°
P,Oi9 + 6ROH ——> 2(RO)PO(OH),
+2(RO),PO(OH)

Under less-controlled conditions P,Oj9 dehy-
drates ethanol to ethene and methylarylcarbinols
to the corresponding styrenes. H2SO, is dehy-
drated to SO3, HNO; gives N2Os and amides

(RCONH?) yield nitriles (RCN). In each of these

P-containing product. PyOjo reacts vigorously
with both wet and dry NH; to form a range of
amorphous polymeric powdery materials which
are used industrially for water softening because
of their ability to sequester Ca ions; composition
depends markedly on the preparative conditions
employed but most of the commercial products
appear to be condensed linear or cyclic amido-
polyphosphates which can be represented by for-
mulae such as:

The annual production/consumption of PyOio in
USA and Western Europe totals about 15000

Other oxides of phosphorus are less well char-
acterized though the suboxide PO and the per-
oxide P2O¢ seem to be definite compounds. PO
was obtained as a brown cathodic deposit when
a saturated solution of Et;NHCI in anhydrous
POCI; was electrolysed between Pt electrodes at
0°. Alternatively it can be made by the slow reac-
tion of POBr; with Mg in Et,O under reflux:

2POBr; + 3Mg ——> 2PO + 3MgBr,

Its structure is unknown but is presumably based
on a polymeric network of P—O-—P links. It reacts
with water to give PH3 and is quantitatively
oxidized to P2Os by oxygen at 300°. The
peroxide P,O.¢ is thought to be the active
ingredient in the violet solid obtained when
P4sOi9 and O2 are passed through a heated

Ch. 12

discharge tube at low pressure. The compound
has not been obtained pure but liberates I; from
aqueous KI, hydrolyses to a peroxophosphoric
acid, and liberates O; when heated to 130°
under reduced pressure. Its structure may be
(O=)2P-O-O-P(=0), or, in view of the
variable composition of the product, it may be
a mixture of PO, and Ps0,2 obtained by
replacing P—O-P links by P-O—O-P in P4Ojo.

Sulfides"")

The sulfides of phosphorus form an intriguing
series of compounds which continue to present
puzzling structural features. The compounds
PsSio, PaSo, PaS7, a-P4Ss, B-P4Ss, a-PsSa, B-
P4S4, P4S3 and P4S2 are all based on the Pg
tetrahedron but only P4Sio (and possibly P4S9)
is structurally analogous to the oxide. P4S¢ is
conspicuous by its absence. Structural data are
summarized in Fig. 12.15 and some physical
properties are in Table 12.6.

P4S; is the most stable compound in the series
and can be prepared by heating the required
amounts of red P and sulfur above 180° in an
inert atmosphere and then purifying the product
by distillation at 420° or by recrystallization
from toluene. The retention of a P3 ring in the
structure is notable. Its reactions and commercial
application in match manufacture are discussed
on p. 509.

The curious phase relations between phos-
phorus, sulfur and their binary compounds are
worth noting. Because both Py and Sg are sta-
ble molecules the phase diagram, if studied
below 100°, shows only solid solutions with
a simple eutectic at 10° (75 atom % P). By
contrast, When the mixtures are heated above
200° the elements react and an entirely different
phase diagram is obtained; however, as only the
most stable compounds P4S3, PsS7 and PsSio

array and the 4 S atoms form a slightly distorted
square. The 2 P—P bonds are long (as also in P4S3

The structure of B-P,S, has not been determined
by X-ray crystallography but spectroscopic data
indicate the absence of P=S groups and the C,
structure shown in Fig. 12.15 is the only other

P,Ss disproportionates below its mp (2P,Ss
=== P,S3 + P,S7) and so cannot be obtained
directly from the melt. It is best prepared by
irtadiating a solution of P4S3; and S in CS2
solution using a trace of iodine as catalyst. Its
structure is quite unexpected and features a single
exocyclic P=S group and 3 fused heterocycles
containing, respectively, 4, 5 and 6 atoms; there
are 2 short P—P bonds and the 4-membered P3S
ring is almost square planar.

P,S; is the second most stable sulfide (after
P,4S3) and can be obtained by direct reaction of
the elements. Perhaps surprisingly the structure
retains a P—P bond and has two exocyclic P=S
groups. P4So is formed reversibly by heating
P4S7 + 2P4Sio and has the structure shown in
Fig. 12.15.

P.Sio is commercially the most important sul-
fide of P and is formed by direct reaction of liquid
white P, with a slight excess of sulfur above 300°.
It can also be made from byproduct ferrophos-
phorus (p. 480).

he
4FexP + 18FeS, ——> P,So + 26FeS
4FeP + 188 —> P,Sio + 8FeS

It has essentially the same structure as the H form
of P,Ojo and hydrolyses mainly according to the
overall equation

P4Sio + 16H2O ——> 4H3PO, + 10H2S

Presumably intermediate thiophosphoric acids are
first formed and, indeed, when the hydrolysis
is carried out in aqueous NaOH solution at
100°, substantial amounts of the mono- and di-
thiophosphates are obtained. P-S bonds are also
retained during reaction of P4Sjq with alcohols
or phenols and the products formed are used
extensively in industry for a wide variety of

---
# Page 525

§12.3.5

Phosphorus Sulfides in Industry

‘The two compounds of importance are P,S; and P4S19. The former is made on a large scale for use in “strike anywhere”
matches according to a formula evolved by Sévéne and Cahen in France in 1898. The ignition results from the violent
reaction between P4$3 and KCIO3 which is initiated by friction of the match against glass paper (on the side of the box)
or other abrasive material. A typical formulation for the match head is:

 

 

 

 

 

Reactants Fillers (moderators) Adhesives:
KCIO; P4S3 Ground glass Fe203 ZnO Glue Water
20%. 9% 14% N% Te 10% 29%

 

Formulations of this type have completely replaced earlier “strike anywhere’ matches based on (poisonous) white Py,
sulfur, and KCIO3. though “safety matches” still use a match head which is predominantly KC1O3 struck against the side
of the match-box which has been covered with a paste of (non-toxic) red P (49.5%), antimony sulfide (27.6%). Fe2Ox
(1.2%) and gum arabic (21.7%). About 10!'! matches are used annually in the UK alone.

PSio is made on an even larger scale than PyS3 and is the primary source of a very wide range of orgenic P-S
compounds. World production of P4S1o exceeds 250000 tonnes annually of which about half is made in the USA, one-
third in the UK/Europe, and the remaining 30000 tonnes elsewhere (Japan, Romania, the former Soviet Union. Mexico,
etc.). The most important reaction of P4S1o is with alcohols or phenols to give dialky} or diary! dithiophosphoric acids:

P4Sio + 8ROH ——> 4(RO),P(S)SH + 2H2S

The zinc salts of these acids are extensively used as additives to lubricating oils to improve their extreme-pressure prop-
erties. The compounds also act as antioxidants, corrosion inhibitors and detergents. Short-chain dialkyl dithiophosphates
and their sodium and ammonium salts are used as flotation agents for zinc and lead sulfide ores. The methyl and ethyl
derivatives (RO)2P(S)SH and (RO)2P(S)CI are of particular interest in the large-scale manufacture of pesticides such as

parathion, malathion, dimethylparathion, etc.'*3) For example parathion, which first went into production as an insecticide
in Germany in 1947, is made by the following reaction sequence:

(Et0),P(S)SH + Cl ——+ HCI + $ + (EtO))P(S)CI
(EtO), P(S)CI + NaOC6Hy - p- NO2 ———+ (EtO))P(S)OCgH4- p-NO> (parathion)

Methylparathion is the corresponding dimethy) derivative. Later (1952) malathion found favour because of its decreased
toxicity to mammals: it is readily made in 90% yield by the addition of dimethyldithiophosphate to diethylmaleate in the
presence of NEty as a catalyst and hydroquinone as a polymerization inhibitor:

CHCO,Et (MeO), P(S)SCHCO> Et
(Me0)2P(S)SH + || mi (malathion)
CHCO.E | CH;COsEt

The scale of manufacture of these organophosphorus pesticides can be guaged from data referring to the USA annual
production in 1975 (tonnes): methytparathion 46000. parathion 36000 and malathion 16000. In addition, some 15 other
thioorganophosphorus insecticides are manufactured in the USA on a scale exceeding 2000 tonnes pa each.) They act
by inhibiting cholinesterase. thus preventing the natural hydrolysis of the neurotransmitter acetylcholine in the insect.20

applications (see Panel). P4Sio is also widely

used to replace O by S in organic compounds to modes, ‘!!©)

Oxosulfides

When P4Oio and P,Sio are heated in appropriate
proportions above 400°, P,O¢S4 is obtained as
colourless hygroscopic crystals, mp 102°.

3P4,010 + 2P4Si0 ——> 5P406S4

The structure is shown in Fig. 12.15. The
related compound P4O04S¢ is said to be formed
by the reaction of Hj2S with POC at 0°
(A. Besson, 1897) but has not been recently
investigated. An amorphous yellow material of
composition P,04S3 is obtained when a solution
of P4S3 in CS» or organic solvents is oxidized
by dry air or oxygen. Other oxosulfides of
uncertain authenticity such as Pg010Ss have been
reported but their structural integrity has not
been established and they may be mixtures.
However, the following series can be prepared by
appropriate redistribution reactions: P40¢S,, (n =
1-4), P4O.Se, (n = 1-3), P4OsSSe, P4O7S,

12.3.6 Oxoacids of phosphorus and
their salts

The oxoacids of P are more numerous than
those of any other element, and the number of
oxoanions and oxo-salts is probably exceeded
only by those of Si. Many are of great importance
technologically and their derivatives are vitally
involved in many biological processes (p. 528).
Fortunately, the structural principles covering this
extensive array of compounds are very simple

and can be stated as follows:?

(i) All P atoms in the oxoacids and oxoanions
are 4-coordinate and contain at least one
P-O unit (1).

oO
|

KN
a
(ii) All P atoms in the oxoacids have at
least one P—OH group (2a) and this often

occurs in the anions also; all such groups
are ionizable as proton donors (2b).

It follows from these structural principles that
cach P atom is S-covalent. However, the
oxidation state of P is 5 only when it is directly
bound to 4 O atoms; the oxidation state is reduced
by 1 each time a P-OH is replaced by a P-P
bond and by 2 cach time a P-OFT is replaced by

  

Oxoacids of phosphorus and their salts sit

a P-H. Some examples of phosphorus oxoacids
are listed in Table 12.7 together with their
recommended and common names. It will be
seen that the numerous structural types and
the variability of oxidation state pose several
problems of nomenclature which offer a rich
source of confusion in the literature.

The oxoacids of P are clearly very different
structurally from those of N (p. 459) and this
difference is accentuated when the standard
reduction potentials (p. 434) and oxidation-state
diagrams (p. 437) for the two sets of compounds
are compared, Some reduction potentials (E/V)
in acid solution are in Table 12.8") (p. 513)
and these are shown schematically below. together
with the corresponding data for alkaline solutions.

state diagram is in Fig. 12.16 which shows the
dramatic difference to N (p. 438).

During the past few decades hydrated
sodium hypophosphite, NaH»PO..H,, has been
increasingly used as an industrial reducing agent,
particularly for the electroless plating of Ni onto
both metals and non-metals.''?°' This developed.
from an accidental discovery by A. Brenner
and Grace E. Riddel at the National Bureau of
Standards, Washington, in 1944. Acid solutions
(E ~~ —0.40V at pH 4-6 and T > 90°) are used
to plate thick Ni layers on to other metals,
but more highly reducing alkaline solutions (pH
7-10; T 25-50") are used to plate plastics and
other non-conducting materials

HPO. +2H,0 + 20 HPO) + 30H":
E~-1s7V

Typical plating solutions contain 10-30g/
of nickel chloride or sulfaie und 10-SOg)
NaH:PO,; with suitable pump capacities it is
possible to plate up to 10kg Ni per hour from
such a bath (ie. 45m? surface to a thickness
of 254m). Chemical plating is more expensive
than normal electrolytic plating but is competitive
when intricate shapes are being plaied and is
essential for non-conducting substrates. (See also
the use of BH,” in this connection, p. 167.)

hypophosphates

There has been much confusion over the struc-

has long ruled out a monomeric formulation,

meric forms are known: (a) hypophosphoric acid
and hypophosphates in which both P atoms
are identical and there is @ direct PP bond;
(b) isohypophosphoric acid and isohypophos-
phates in which 1 P has # direct P-H bond

and the 2 different P atoms are joined by a
PM_Q-P¥ fink.)

(Other lower oxoacids of phosphorus

The possibility of P-H and P-P_ bonds
in phosphorus oxoacids, coupled with the
ease of polymerization via P-O-P linkages
cnables innumerable acids and their salts
to be synthesized. Frequently mixtures arc
‘obtained and these can be separated by paper
chromatography, paper electrophoresis, thin-
layer chromatography, ion exchange or gel
chromatography.('78) Much ingenuity has been
expended in designing appropriate syntheses but
no new principles emerge. A few examples are
listed in Table 12.9 to illustrate both the range
of compounds available and also the abbreviated
notation, which proves to be more convenient
than formal systematic nomenclature in this area,
In this notation the sequence of P-P and P-O-P
links is indicated and the oxidation state of each P
is shown as a superscript numeral which enables
the full formula (including P-H groups) to be
deduced.

The phosphoric acids

‘This section deals with orthopbosphoric acid
(4202), pyrophosphoric acid (HsP20r) and the
polyphosphoric acids (Hy :2PnO3e4)- Several
‘of these compounds can be isolated pure but
their facile interconversion renders this area
of phosphorus chemistry far more complex

than might otherwise appear. The corresponding

Orthophosphoric acid is a remarkable sub-
stance: it can only be obtained pure in the
crystalline state (mp 42.35°C) and when fused
it slowly undergoes partial self-dehydration to
diphosphoric acid:

2H3PO4, == H20 + H4P207

The sluggish equilibrium is obtained only after
several weeks near the mp but is more rapid at
higher temperatures. This process is accompanied
by extremely rapid autoprotolysis (see below)
which gives rise to several further (ionic) species
in the melt. As the concentration of these various
species builds up the mp slowly drops until at
equilibrium it is 34.6°, corresponding to about 6.5
mole% of diphosphate.“ Slow crystallization
of stoichiometric molecular H;PO, from this
isocompositional melt gradually reverses the
equilibria and the mp eventually rises again to the
initial value. Crystalline H;PO4 has a hydrogen-
bonded layer structure in which each PO(OH);
molecule is linked to 6 others by H bonds which
are of two lengths, 253 and 284 pm. The shorter
bonds link OH and O=P groups whereas the
longer H bonds are between 2 OH groups on
adjacent molecules.

 

Extensive H bonding persists on fusion and
phosphoric acid is a viscous syrupy liquid that

"9'N.N. GREENWooD and A. THOMPSON, J. Chem. Soc.
3485-92 and 3864-7 (1959).

Ch. 12

readily supercools. At 45°C (just above the mp)
the viscosity is 76.5 centipoise (cP) and this
increases to 177.7cP at 25°. These values can
be compared with 1.00cP for H2O at 20° and
24.5 cP for anhydrous H2SO, at 25°. As shown
in the Table“? trideuterophosphoric acid has an
even higher viscosity and deuteration also raises
the mp and density.

concentration of 0.54 molal for HgPO4*. These
values arc about 20-30 times greater than the

THSO, | 0.0178 molal, [HySO¢*} 0.0135 molal
and [HS,O7~] 0.0088 molal (see p. 711). Be-
cause of the very high viscosity of molten
HPO, electrical conduction by normal ionic
migration is negligible and the high conductivity
is due almost entirely to @ rapid. proton-switch
followed by 2 relatively slow reorientation
involving the H2PO,~ ion, H-bonded to the
solvent structure (Fig. 12.17). Note that
the tetrahedral H4PO,* ion, ie. [P(OH)4)*.
like the NH,* ion in liquid NHs, does
not contribute to the proton-switch conduction
mechanism in HyPO, because, having no dipole
moment, it does not orient preferentially in the
applied electric field; accordingly any proton
switching will occur randomly in all directions
independently of the applied field and therefore
will not contribute to the electrical conduction,
Addition of the appropriate amount of
water 10 anhydrous HyPO,, or crystallization

Industrial production and uses of H3PO04°758-9.11.132)

Phosphoric acid‘!3”) is manufactured on a vast scale and is produced in a wide variety of concentrations and purities. It
is therefore convenient to express production figures in terms of the amount of contained PsOjo (the figures based on the
equivalent amount of contained anhydrous H3PO, can be obtained by multiplying by the factor 1.380, though these may
be misleading if they are taken to imply that it is the anhydrous acid that is being produced). World production capacity
in 1986 exceeded 43 million tonnes of contained PsO;o and was distributed as follows:

Production capacity of phosphoric acid (million tonnes/year of contained PsO10)

 

 

North USSR & Western Asia and Central/S. Middle
Region America East.Eur. Africa Europe Australasia America East
“PsOio"/Mtpa 31 10.6 6.1 5.0 39 24 1s

 

 

 

Production is still increasing steadily in many countries “Thermal” acid (made by oxidation of phosphorus in the presence
of water vapour) is about 3 times as expensive as “wet” acid (made by treating rock phosphate with sulfuric acid). The
present approximate pattern of production and uses is shown in the following scheme:

 

  

 

 

 

 

 

Ch. 12

ph: >) (impure) ! Fenilizers

phosphorus (pure) Detergent phosphates
Pharmaceuticals
20% 20%
v v
Phosphorus sulfides Metal treatment
Phosphorus chlorides _(€.g. pickling, cleaning,
Organic P compounds —_rust-proofing, polishing)

Many of these uses have already been discussed, or will be in later sections (pp. 524, 527).

Applications of phosphoric acid in metal treatment date from 1869 when a British patent was granted for the prevention
of rusting of corset stays by damp air or perspiration. Improvements followed the incorporation of certain metal ions in
the phosphatizing solution (notably Mn, Fe and Zn), and today corrosion resistance is imparted in this way to innumerable
metal objects such as nuts, bolts, screws, tools, car-engine parts, gears, etc. In addition, car-bodies, refrigerators, washing
machines and other electrical applicances with painted or enamelled surfaces all use phosphatized undercoatings to prevent
the paint from blistering or peeling. The simple immersion process may take up to 2 h at 90°C but can be accelerated
25-fold by adding small amounts of oxidizing agent such as NaNOs and Cu(NOs)2. A zine phosphatized coating is usually
about 0.6 4m thick (ie, 22gm~*), Another important process is “bright dip” or chemical polishing of Al metal which
has replaced chrome plating for car trims and other uses: the metal is immersed at 91~99°C in a solution containing 95
parts by weight of 85% H3PO,, 4 parts of 68% HNO, and 0.01% Cu(NOs)p, followed by electrolytic anodization to
give the mirror-like surface a protective coating of transparent Al:Os.

Polyphosphoric acid supported on diatomaceous earth (p. 342) is a petrochemicals catalyst for the polymerization,
alkylation, dehydrogenation, and low-temperature isomerization of hydrocarbons. Phosphoric acid is also used in the
production of activated carbon (p. 274). In addition to its massive use in the fertilizer industry (p. 524) free phosphoric
acid can be used as a stabilizer for clay soils: small additions of H3PO, under moist conditions gradually leach out Al
and Fe from the clay and these form polymeric phosphates which bind the clay particles together. An allied, though more
refined use is in the setting of dental cements.

By far the greatest consumption of pure aqueous phosphoric acid is in the preparation of various salts for use in the
food, detergent and tooth-paste industries (p. 524). When highly diluted the free acid is non-toxic and devoid of odour,
and is extensively used to impart the sour or tart taste to many soft drinks (“carbonated beverages”) such as the various
colas (~0,05% H3PO,, pH 2.3), root beers (~0.01% H3POs. pH 5.0), and sarsaparilla (~0,01% H3POx, pH ~4.5).

 

 

'2p. BeckER, Phosphates and Phosphoric Acid, Marcel Dekker, New York, 1988, 760 pp.

orange (pK; 3.5) and the second, corresponding
to NayHPO,, is indicated by the phenolphthalein
end point (pK; 9.5). The third equivalence point
cannot be detected directly by means of a
coloured indicator. Between the two inflexions
the pH changes relatively slowly with addition of
NaOH and this is an example of buffer action.*
Indeed, one of the standard buffer solutions used
in analytical chemistry comprises an equimolar
mixture of NagHPO4, and KH2PO,. Another
important buffer, which has been designed to
have a pH close to that of blood, consists of
0.03043 M NagHPO, and 0.008695 M KH2PO,,
i.e. a mole ratio of 3.5:1 (pH 7.413 at 25°).
Concentrated H3PO, is one of the major acids
of the chemical industry and is manufactured on

+A buffer solution is one that resists changes in pH on
dilution or on addition of acid or alkali, It consists of a
solution of a weak acid (e.g. H2POs~) and its conjugate
base (HPO4?~) and is most effective when the concentration
of the two species are the same. For example at 25° an
equimolar mixture of NayHPO, and KH2PO4 has pH 6.654
when each is 0.2m and pH 6.888 when each is 0.01 mM.
The central section of Fig. 12.18 shows the variation in pH
of an equimolar buffer of NayHPO, and NaH PO, at a
concentration of 0.033M (you should check this statement).
Further discussion of buffer solutions is given in standard
textbooks of volumetric analysis.

the multimillion-tonne scale for the production
of phosphate fertilizers and for many other
purposes (see Panel). Two main processes (the
so-called “thermal” and “wet” processes) are used
depending on the purity required. The “thermal”
(or “furnace”) process yields concentrated acid
essentially free from impurities and is used
in applications involving products destined for
human consumption (see also p. 524); in this
process a spray of molten phosphorus is burned
in a mixture of air and steam in a stainless steel
combustion chamber:

The somewhat older “wet” (or “gypsum”) process
involves the treatment of rock phosphate (p. 476)
with sulfuric acid, the idealized stoichiometry

10~!°, and the corresponding negative logarithms
are: pK; ~ 1.0, pK2 ~ 1.8, pK3 6.57 and pK4
9.62. The P—O—P linkage is kinetically stable

Orthophosphates 3:54)

Phosphoric acid forms several series of salts
in which the acidic H atoms are successively
replaced by various cations; there is considerable
commercial application for many of these
compounds.

Lithium orthophosphates are unimportant and
differ from the other alkali metal phosphates in
being insoluble. At least 10 crystalline hydrated
or anhydrous sodium orthophosphates are known
and these can be grouped into three series:

NaPOq.nH20 (n = 0, 3, 6, 8, 12)

Na,HPO,.nH2O (n = 0, 2, 7, 8, 12)

NaH PO4.nH30 (n = 0, 1, 2),
NaH»PO4.H3PO, [i.e. NaHs(PO4)2]
NaHpPO4.Na>HPO, [i.e. NasH3(POs)2]
and 2NaH2PO4.NagHPO4.2H20

Likewise, there are at least 10 well-characterized
potassium orthophosphates and several ammo-
nium analogues. The presence of extensive H
bonding in many of these compounds leads to
considerable structural complexity and frequently
confers important properties (see later). The

mono- and di-sodium phosphates are prepared
industrially by neutralization of aqueous H3PO4
with soda ash (anhydrous NayCOs3, p. 89). How-
ever, preparation of the trisodium salts requires
the use of the more expensive NaOH to replace
the third H atom. Careful control of concen-
tration and temperature are needed to avoid
the simultaneous formation of pyrophosphates
(diphosphates). Some indication of the struc-
tural complexity can be gained from the com-
pound Na3PO04.12H2O which actually crystallizes
with variable amounts of NaOH up to the lim-
iting composition 4(Na3PO4.12H2O).NaOH. The
structure is built from octahedral [Na(H20).]
units which join to form “hexagonal” rings of
6 octahedra which in turn form a continuous
two-dimensional network of overall composition
{Na(H,0),}; between the sheets lie {PO4} con-
nected to them by H bonds.) Some industrial,
domestic, and scientific applications of Na, K and
NHg orthophosphates are given in the Panel.

Calcium orthophosphates are _ particularly
important in fertilizer technology, in the chem-
istry of bones and teeth, and in innumerable
industrial and domestic applications (see Panel).
They are also the main source of phospho-
rus and phosphorus chemicals and occur in
vast deposits as apatites and rock phosphate
(p. 475). The main compounds occurring in
the CaO-H2O0-P4O;9 phase diagram are: Ca-
(H2PO,4)2, Ca(H2PO4)2.H20, Ca(HPO,).nH,O
(n =0, 5,2), Cas(PO4)2, CarPO4(OH).2H20,
Cas(PO4)3OH (i.e. apatite), CagP209 [probably
Ca3(PO4)2.CaO] and CagH2(PO4)s.5H20.

In all of these alkali-metal and alkaline
earth-metal orthophosphates there are discrete,
approximately regular tetrahedral PO, units in

Uses of Orthophosphates®

Phosphates are used in an astonishing variety of dontestic and industial applications but their ubiquitous presence and
thei substanial impact on everyday life is frequently overlooked. I will be convenient fist (0 indicate the specific uses
‘f individual compounds and the properties on which they are based. then to conclude with a brief summary of many
<ifferent types of application and their interreiation. The most widely used compounds are the various phosphate sats of
Na, K, NH and Ca, The uses of di. ui- and poly-phosphates are mentioned on pp. 527-

'NaxPO, is strongly alkaline in aqueous solution and is thus a valuable constituent of scouring powders. paint strippers
and grease saponitirs. Its complex with NuOCI [(Na3POs.11Hs0}y.NaOC, is also strongly alkaline (a 19 solution has
pH 11.8) and, in addition. i releases active chlonne when weited: this combination of scouring, bleaching and bacteriocidal
‘ction makes the adduct valuable in formulations of automatic dishwashing powders.

NazHPOs is widely used as a buffer component (p. 521). The use ofthe dihydrate (~2% concentrauon) 3s an emulsiier
in the manuiacture of pasieunzed processed cheese was patented by JL, Kraft in 1916 and is still used, together wath
insoluble sodium metaphosphate or the mixed phosphate NaysAly(PO: x. to process cheese on the multikilotonne scale
daily. Despite much study. the reason why phosphate salts act as emulsifiers is ill pot well understood in detail. Na;HPOs
is also added (0.1%) 10 evaporated milk to maintain the correct CaPO, balance and 1 prevent gelation of the milk
powder toa mush. Its addion at the 5% level to brine (|S~20% NaC! solution) forthe pickling of ham makes the product
more tender and juicy by preventing the exudauon of juices during subsequent cooking. Another major use in the food
Industry is as a starch modifier: small additions enhance the ability wo form stable cold-water gels (e.g. instant podding
mixes). and the addition of 1% to farinaceous products raises the pH 10 slightly above 7 and provides “quick-cooking™
breakfast cereals.

‘NalPO4 18 a solid water-solubte acid, and this property finds use (with NaHCOs) in effervescent laxative tables and
in the pH adjustment of boiler waters. 1 is also used ay a mild phosphatizing agent for stel surfaces and as a constituent

the undercoat for metal pains.

K POs (like Nay POs) is strongly alkaline in aqueous solution and is used to absorb HaS from gas streams; the solution
‘can be regenerated simply by heating. K:POr is als used as a regulating electrolye fo conto! the stability of synthetic
latex during the polymenzation of styrenebutadiene rubbers, The buffering action of KsHPO« has already been mentioned
{p. 321) and this is the reson for iis addition as a corrosion inhibitor 10 car-rdvator coolants which otherwise tend to
become acidic due to slow oxidation ofthe glycol antifreeze. KHPOs isa piezoelectric (p. 57) and finds use in submarine
‘sonar sysiems. For many applications, however. the cheaper sodium salts are preferred untess there isa specific advantage
forthe potassium salt; one example isthe specialist balanced commercial ferulwzer formulation (KH2POx.(NH4 )2HPOs]
which contains 10.54, N, 53% P:Os and 17.2% Kz0 (ie. N-P-K 10-53-17),

(NHla):HPOs and (NHOH:POq can be used interchangeably as specialist fertilizers end nutrients in fermentation
broths; though expensive, their high concentration of active ingredients ameliorate this. particularly in localities where
{transportation costs are high. Indeed. (NH)zHPOs in granulated or ligaid form consumes more phosphate rock than any
‘ther single end-product (over 8 million tonnes pa in the USA alone in 1974). Ammonwm phosphates are also much
used as flame retardants for cellulosic materials, about 3-54 gain in dry weight being the optimum treatment, Their
‘action probably depends on their ready dissociation into NH and HsPOs on heating, the HsPOx then catalyses the
decomposition of cellulose to a siow-burning char (carbon) and thus, together with the suppression of flammable volatiles,
smothers the flame. As the ammonium phosphates are soluble, they are used mainly for cunains, theatre scenery and
disposable paper dresses and costumes. The related compound urea phosphate (NH:CONH?.HyPQg) has also been used
to flameproof cotion fabrics: the materia is soaked in a concentrated aqueous solution, dried (154 weight gain and cured
‘at 160° to bond the retardant to the cellulose fibre. The advantage is thatthe retardant does not wash out, But the strength
of the fabric is somewhal reduced by the process.

Calcium phosphates have a broad range of applications both in the food indusiry and as bulk feritizers. The vast scale
‘of the pbosphate rock industry has already been indicated (p. 476) and this is further elaborated for the particular case of
the USA in the Scheme on the page opposite (kilotonnes pa and ‘%, 1974)

‘The crucial importance of Ca and POs as nutrient supplements for the healthy growth of bones, teeth, muscle and
nerve cells has long been recognized. The non-cellular bone structure of an average adult human consists of ~60% of
some form of “iricalcium phosphate” (Cas(POs)3OH|; teth likewise comprise ~70% and average persons cary A5kg
‘of this materal in their bodies. Phosphates in the body are replenished by a continuous cycle, and used P is eared by
the blood to the kidneys and then excreted in urine, mainly as Na(NH)HPOs, An average adult eliminates 3~43 of POL
equivalent daly (ef. the discovery of P in unine by Brandl, p. 473).

Calcium phosphates are used in baking acids, oothpastes, mineral supplements and stock feeds. Ca(HPOx)2 was
introduced as a leavening acid inthe late nineteenth century (to replace “cream of tartar” KHC:HsOs) but the monohydrate
(inwroduced in the 19303) finds more use today. "Simight baking powder", a mixture of Ca(H3PO4)2.H20 and NaliCOy
with some 40% starch coating. tends to produce CO> too quickly during dough mixing and so “combination baking

which P—O is usually in the range 153 + 3pm
and the angle O—P—O is usually in the range
109 + 5°. Extensive H-bonding and M~O inter-
actions frequently induce substantial deviations
from a purely ionic formulation (p. 81). This
trend continues with the orthophosphates of
tervalent elements MPO, (M=B, Al, Ga,
Cr, Mn, Fe) which all adopt structures closely
related to the polymorphs of silica (p. 342).
NaBePO, is similar, and YPOq4 adopts the zir-
con (ZrSiO4) structure. The most elaborate anal-
ogy so far revealed is for AlPO, which can
adopt each of the 6 main polymorphs of sil-
ica as indicated in the scheme below. The
analogy covers not only the structural relations
between the phases but also the sequence of
transformation temperatures (°C) and the fact
that the o-~f-transitions occur readily whilst
the others are sluggish (p. 343). Similarly, the
orthophosphates of B, Ga and Mn are known
in the f-quartz and the @- and f-cristobalite
forms whereas FePO, adopts either the a- or
#-quartz structure. Numerous hydrated forms
are also known. The Al-PO,—H2O system is
used industrially as the basis for many adhe-
sives, binders and cements.“35) Novel chain

 

and sheet aluminium phosphate anions of com-
position [H2AIP20g] and [AlsP4O\6]*~, respec-
tively, have also recently been structurally
characterized.(6)

Chain polyphosphates 23.5)

A rather different structure-motif is observed in
the chain polyphosphates: these feature corner-
shared {PO,} tetrahedra as in the polyphosphoric
acids (p. 522). The general formula for such
anions is [PrOsn4i]*2-, of which the
diphosphates, P2074~, and tripolyphosphates,
P30;0°~, constitute the first two members. Chain
polyphosphates have been isolated with n up to
10 and with n “infinite”, but those of intermediate
chain length (10 < n < 50) can only be obtained
as glassy or amorphous mixtures, As the chain
length increases, the ratio (3n + 1)/n approaches
3.00 and the formula approaches that of the
polymetaphosphates [PO3~ ]oo.

Diphosphates (pyrophosphates) are usually
prepared by thermal condensation of dihydrogen

Many diphosphates of formula M'YP30;, M!-
PO; and hydrated M}P3O7 are known and there
has been considerable interest in the relative
orientation of the two linked (POs) groups and
in the P-O-P angle between them." For
small cations the 2 (POs) are approximately
staggered whereas for larger cations they tend to
be nearly eclipsed. The P-O-P angle is large and
variable, ranging from 130° in NasP207.10H20_
to 156° in a-Mg>P30>. The apparent colinearity
in the higher-temperature (#) form of many
diphosphates, which was previously ascrihed
to a P-O-P angle of 180°, is now generally
attributed to positional disorder. Bridging P-O
distances are invariably longer than terminal P-O.
distances, typical values (for NasP:07.10H20)
being P-O,, 161 pm, P-O, 152pm. Note that
bridging can also be via a peroxo group
as in ammonium peroxodiphosphate'!™*! which
features the 7ig-zag anion {0;P-O-O~POs]*
with P-O,, 165.8pm, P-O, 150.8 pm and 0-0.

Before the advent of synthetic detergents,
NayP207 was much used as a dispersant for lime
soap scum which formed in hard water, but it
has since been replaced by the tripolyphosphate
(see below). However, the ability of diphosphate
ions to form a gel with soluble calcium salts
has made NasP2O; a useful ingredient for starch-
type instant pudding which requires no cooking.
The main application of NazH2P20) is as a
Jeavening acid in baking: it does not react with
NaHCOy until heated, and so large batches of
dough or batter can be made up and stored.
CapP207, because of its insolubility, inertness,
and abrasive properties, is used as a toothpaste
additive compatible with Sn"! and fluoride ions.
(see Panel on p. 525)

Of the tripolyphosphates only the sodium salt
need be mentioned. It was introduced in the mid-
1940s as a “builder” for synthetic detergents, and
its production for this purpose is now measured
in megatonnes per annum (see Panel on the next
page). On the industrial scale NasP:Oyo is usually
made by heating an intimate mixture of pow-
dered Na;HPOs and NaHyPO; of the required
stoichiometry under carefully controlled condi-
tions:

2Na:HPO, + NaH,PO; —> NacP Oyo + 2H;

 

> NajH2P207

 

‘The low-temperature form (I) converts to the
high-temperature form (II) above 417°C and both
forms react with water to give the crystalline
hexahydrate. All three materials contain the

528 Phosphorus Ch. 12

Uses of Sodium Tripolyphosphate

Many synthetic detergents contain 25-45% NasP3Oy though the amount is lower in the USA than in Europe because of
the problems of eutrophication in some areas (p. 478) It acts mainly a a water softener. by chelating and sequestering
the Mg?* and C3?* in hard water. Indeed, the formation constants of its complexes with these ions are nearly one million-
{fold greater than with Na*: (NaPsOw*” pk ~2.8; MgP30,0>” pK ~8.6; CaP,O1o"~ pK ~8.1). In addition, NasP:O10
increases the efficiency of the surfactant by lowering the critical micelle Concentration, and by its ability to suspend and
‘peptize dirt particles by building up a large negative charge on the particles by adsorption: it also furnishes 2 suitable
‘alkalinity for cleansing action without irritating eyes or skin and it provides effective buflering action at these pHs, The
dramatic growth of sytheic detergert powders during the 1950s was secompanied by an equally drametic drop inthe
Use of soap powders.(17

'NasP3Oho is also used as a dispersing agent in clay suspensions used in oil-well drilling. Again, addition of <1%
'NasP30i0 10 the shuries used in manufacturing cement and bricks enables much less water to be used to attain workability,
‘and thus less to be removed during the seiting or calcining processes.

12.3.7 Phosphorus-nitrogen
compounds

The PN bond is one of the most intriguing in
chemistry and many of its more subtle aspects

still clude a detailed and satisfactory description.
t occurs in innumerable compounds, frequently
of great stability, and in many of these the
strength of the bond and the shortness of the
interatomic distance have been interpreted in
terms of “partial double-bond character". In
fact, the conventional symbols P-N and P=-N
are more an aid to electron counting than a
description of the bond in any given compound
(sce p. 538).

Many compounds containing the P-N link
can be considered formally as derivatives
‘of the oxcacids of phosphorus and their
salts (pp. 510-31) in which there has been
isoclectronic replacement of

 

PH [or PCOH)] by P(NH2) or PONR2):
P=0 for P=S]_ by P=NH_ or Ps NR;

---
# Page 548

532 Phosphorus

P-O-P by P-NH-P_ or P-NR-P,

ete.)

Examples are phosphoramidic acid, HyNP(O)-
(OF)2; phosphordiamidic acid, (HpN)2P(OOED;
phosphoric triamide, (H2N),PO; and their
derivatives. ‘There are an enormous number
of compounds featuring the 4-coordinate group
shown in structure (1) including the versatile
Ronaqueous solvent hexamethylphosphoramide
(MeN),PO; this is readily made by reacting
POCK with 6Me.NH, and dissolves metallic Na
to give paramagnetic blue solutions similar to
those in liquid NH (p. 77).

 

Another scries includes the cyclo-metaphos-
Phimic acids, which are tautomers of the cyclo-
polyphosphazenc hydroxides (p. 541). Similarly.
halogen atoms in PX3 or other P- X compounds:
can be successively replaced by the isoelectronic
groups -NHz, -NHR, —NRz, ete., and some-
limes a pair of halogens can be replaced by = NH
or °=NR. These, in turn, can be used to prepare
a large number of other derivatives as indicated
schematically opposite for P(NMe)x?).
Although such compounds all formally con-
tain P-N single bonds, they frequently display
properties consistent with more extensive bond-
ing. A particularly clear example is PF2(NMe2)

Polyphosphazenes

The grouping

R
\
—N=P— is isoelectronic with the

R
R

\
silicone grouping —O—Si— (p. 364)
\

R

and, after the silicones, the polyphosphazenes
form the most extensive series of cova-
lently bonded polymers with a non-carbon
skeleton. This section will describe their

applications, 28.160.161)

Preparation and structure. Polyphosphazenes
have a venerable history. (NPCI2), oligomers
were first made in 1834 by J. von Liebig
and F, Wohler who reacted PCls with NH,
but their stoichiometry and structure were not
elucidated until much later. The fluoro analogues
(NPF)), were first made in 1956 and the bromo
compounds (NPBr2), in 1960. The synthesis of
(NPCl)), was much improved by R. Schenk and
G. Rémer in 1924 and their method remains
the basis for present-day production on both the
laboratory and industrial scales:

solvent

nPCls + nNH,Cl Tous (NPCI;),, + 4nHCl

Appropriate solvents are 1,1,2,2-tetrachloro-
ethane (bp 146°), PhCl (bp 132°) and 1,2-
dichlorobenzene (bp 179°). By varying the
conditions, yields of the cyclic trimer or tetramer
and other oligomers can be optimized and
the compounds then separated by fractionation.
Highly polymeric (NPClz).. can be made by
heating cyclo-(NPCl,)3 to 150-300°, though
heating to 350° induces depolymerization.
Polycyclic compounds are rarely obtained in

5123.7

these preparations, one exception being NPsCly,
mp 237.5", which can be obtained in modest
yields from the direct thermolytic reaction of
PCls and NHgCl. The tricyclic structure is
strongly distorted from planarity though the
central NP group features an accurately planar 3-
coordinate N atom with much longer N--P bonds
than those in the peripheral macrocycle. The 2
sorts of P Cl bonds are also noticeably different
in length and the 3 central Cl atoms are all on
tone side of the NP} plane with ZNPCI 104”,

 

Many details of the preparative reaction
mechanism remain unclear but it is thought
that NH,CI partly dissociates into NH3 and
HCI, and that PCls reacts in its ionic form
PChy*PCig~ (p. 499). Nucleophilic attack by
NH3 on PCl* then occurs with elimination
of HCI and the [HN==PCly} attacks a second
PCLy* to give [ClhP-=N-PCly]* and HCI. After
Th the major (insoluble) intermediate product
is [ClsP~N- PCIs]*PCle” (i.e. PyNChi2, p. 536)
and this then slowly reacts with more NHy
to give HCI and (ChP=N-PC=NH}, etc.
It is probable that NHsBr and PBr,*Br- react
similarly to give (NPBr2), but NHqF fluorinates
PCIs to NH,PF, and the fluoroanalogues (NPF2)y
are best prepared by fluorinating (NPCl), with
KSO2F/SQ; (i.c. KF in liguid SO2). Similarly,
standard substitution reactions lead to many
cerivatives in which all (or some) of the CI
atoms are replaced by OMe, OFt, OCH2CF:
OPh, NHPh, NMe2, NR. R, Ar, etc. Partial

 

 

 

Phosphorus-nitrogen compounds 537

replacement leads to geminal derivatives (in
which both Cl atoms on 1 P atom ate replaced)
and to non-geminal derivatives which, in turn,
ccan exist as cis- oF frans- isomers.

The cyclic trimer (NPF2)s, mp 28°, has
an accurately planar 6-membered ring (Dsy
symmetry) in which all 6 P—N distances are
‘equal (156 pm) and the angles NPN and PNP
are all 120 1°. Most other trimers ase also
more-or-less planar with equal P -N distances:
for example, (NPCl2); is almost planar (pseudo-
chair with P-N 158pm, P-Cl 197pm, ZNPN

ZPNP 121.4", CIPCI 102°. Perhaps
tetramer (NPF2)s, mp
also a planar heterocycle (Dy symmetry)
sn shorter PN bonds (151 pm) and with
gles of 122.7° and 147.4° at P and N
ly. However, other conformations are
found in other derivatives, c.g, chair (C2s), saddle
(D2), boat (S4), crown tetrameric (C4) and
hybrid. Thus (NPCh); exists in the metastable
K form (in which it has the boat conformation)
and the stable T form (chair configuration) as
shown in Fig. 12.24. The remarkable diversity
of molecular conformations observed for the 8-
membered heterocycle (P<Nz) suggests that the

4-coordinate P. The experimental facts that have
to be interpreted by any acceptable theory of
bonding are:

  

the rings and chains are very stable;

Gi) the skeletal interatomic distances are
equal around the ring (or along the chain)
unless there is differing substitution at the
various P atoms;

(ili) the P-N distances are shorter than
expected for a covalent single bond
(177 pm) and are usually in the range

158 2 pm (though bonds as short as 147
pm occur in some compounds):

(iv) the N-P-N angles are usually in the
range 120 + 2° but the P -N--P angles in
various compounds span the range from
120- 148.6";

(¥) skeletal N atoms are weakly basic and can
be protonated or form coordination com-
plexes, especially when there are electron
releasing groups on P;

(vi) unlike many aromatic systems the phos

phazene skeleton is hard to reduce elec-

trochemically;

 

(vii) spectral effects associated with organic 1-
systems (such as the bathochromic ultra~
violet shift that accompanies increased
electron delocalization) are not found.

In short, the bonding in phosphazenes is not ade-
quately represented by a sequence of alternating
double and single bonds -N= Pe yet it

differs from aromatic o—z system in which there
is extensive electron delocalization via pr—Pr
bonding. The possibility of px—d, bonding in
N-P systems has been considered by many
authors since the mid-1950s but there is still
no consensus, and for nearly every argument
that can be mounted in favour of P(3d)-orbital
contributions another can be raised against it.

It seems generally agreed that 2 electrons on

N occupy an sp? lone-pair in the plane of the
ring (or the plane of the local PNP triangle) as
in Fig. 12.26a. The situation at P is less clear
mainly because of uncertainties concerning the
d-orbital energies and the radial extent (size) of
these orbitals in the bonding situation (as distinct
from the free atom). In so far as symmetry is
concerned, the sp? lone-pair on each N can be
involved in coordinate bonding in the xy plane

Applications. Many applications have been
proposed for polyphosphazenes, _ particularly
the non-cyclic polymers of high molecular
weight, but those with the most desirable
properties are extremely expensive and costs
will have to drop considerably before they
gain widespread use (cf. silicones, p. 365).
The cheapest compounds are the chloro series

Ch. 12

(NPCIz),, but these readily hydrolyse in moist
air to polymetaphosphimic acids. Greater stability
is displayed by amino, alkoxy, phenoxy and
especially fluorinated derivatives, and these are
attracting increasing interest as rigid plastics,
elastomers, plastic films, extruded fibres and
expanded foams.''!7 Such materials (MW
>500000) are water-repellent, solvent-resistant,
fiame-resistant and flexible at low temperatures
(Fig. 12.30). Possible applications are as fuel
hoses, gaskets and O-ring seals for use in
high-flying aircraft or for vehicles in Arctic
climates. Their extraordinary dielectric strength
makes them good candidates for metal coatings
and wire insulation. Other applications of
polyphosphazenes include their use to improve
the high-temperature properties of phenolic resins
and their use as composites with asbestos or glass
for non-flammable insulating material. Some
of the more reactive derivatives have been
proposed as pesticides and even as ultra-high
capacity fertilizers.

12.3.8 Organophosphorus
compounds

A general treatment of the vast domain of
organic compounds of phosphorus‘’”) falls out-
side the scope of this book though several
important classes of compound have already
been briefly mentioned, e.g. tertiary phosphine
ligands (p. 494), alkoxyphosphines and_ their
derivatives (p. 496), organophosphorus halides
(p. 500), phosphate esters in life processes
(p. 528) and organic derivatives of PN com-
pounds (preceding section). There are also
innumerable organic derivatives of the poly-
cyclic polyphosphanes (p. 495), 7.72) and vast
numbers of heterocyclic organophosphorus

compounds.“73:!74) Within the general realm

Many organophosphorus compounds are highly
toxic and frequently lethal. They have been
actively developed for herbicides, pesticides and
more sinister purposes such as nerve gases which
disorient, harass, paralyse or kill.

13

Arsenic,

13.1 Introduction

The three elements arsenic, antimony and
bismuth, which complete Group 15 of the periodic
table, were amongst the earliest elements to be
isolated and all were known before either nitrogen
(1772) or phosphorus (1669) had been obtained
as the free elements. The properties of arsenic
sulfide and related compounds have been known
to physicians and professional poisoners since the
fifth century BC though their use is no longer
recommended by either group of practitioners.
Isolation of the element is sometimes credited
to Albertus Magnus (AD 1193-1280) who heated
orpiment (As2S3) with soap, and its name
reflects its ancient lineage. [Arsenic, Latin
arsenicum from Greek &poeviKdv (arsenicon)
which was itself derived (with addition of 6v)
from Persian az-zarnikh, yellow orpiment (zar =
gold).] Antimony compounds were also known
to the ancients and the black sulfide, stibnite, was
used in early biblical times as a cosmetic to darken
and beautify women’s eyebrows; a rare Chaldean
vase of cast antimony dates from 4000 BC and
antimony-coated copper articles were used in
Egypt 2500-2200 Bc. Pliny (~AD 50) gave it

547

Antimony and Bismuth

the name stibium and writings attributed to Jabir
(~AD 800) used the form antimonium;, indeed,
both names were used for both the element
and its sulfide until the end of the eighteenth
century (Lavoisier). The history of the element,
like that of arsenic, is much obscured by the
intentionally vague and misleading descriptions
of the alchemists, though the elusive Benedictine
monk Basil Valentine may have prepared it in
1492 (about the time of Columbus). N. Lémery
published his famous Treatise on Antimony in
1707. Bismuth was known as the metal at
least by 1480 though its previous history in
the Middle Ages is difficult to unravel because
the element was sometimes confused with Pb,
Sn, Sb or even Ag. The Gutenberg printing
presses (1440 onwards) used type that had been
cut from brass or cast from Pb, Sn or Cu,
but about 1450 a secret method of casting
type from Bi alloys came into use and this
particular use is still an important application of
the element (p. 549). The name derives from the
German Wismut (possibly white metal or meadow
mines) and this was latinized to bisemutum by
the sixteenth-century German scientist G. Bauer
(Agricola) about 1530. Despite the difficulty of

---
# Page 564

548

assigning precise dates to discoveries made by
alchemists, miners and metal workers (or indeed
even discerning what those discoveries actually
were), it seems clear that As, Sb and Bi became
increasingly recognized in their free form during
the thirteenth to fifteenth centuries; they are
therefore contemporary with Zn and Co, and
predate all other elements except the 7 metals
and 2 non-metallic elements known from ancient
times (Au, Ag, Cu, Fe, Hg, Pb, Sn; C and S$).

Arsenic and antimony are classed as metalloids
or semi-metals and bismuth is a typical B sub-
group (post-transition-element) metal like tin and
lead.

13.2 The Elements

13.2.1 Abundance, distribution
and extraction

None of the three elements is particularly abun-
dant in the earth’s crust though several minerals
contain them as major constituents. As can be
seen from Table 13.1, arsenic occurs about half-
way down the elements in order of abundance,
grouped with several others near 2 ppm. Anti-
mony has only one-tenth of this abundance and
Bi, down by a further factor of 20 or more, is
about as unabundant as several of the commoner
platinum metals and gold. In common with all the
post-transition-element metals, As, Sb and Bi are
chalcophiles, i.e. they occur in association with
the chalcogens S, Se and Te rather than as oxides
and silicates.

Arsenic minerals are widely distributed
throughout the world and small amounts of the
free element have also been found. Common

'M.E, Weeks, Discovery of the Elements, Chap. 3,
pp. 91-119, Journal of Chemical Education, Easton, Pa,
1956.

Arsenic, Antimony and Bismuth

Ch. 13

minerals include the two sulfides realgar (As4S4)
and orpiment (As)S3) and the oxidized form
arsenolite (As,03). The arsenides of Fe, Co and
Ni and the mixed sulfides with these metals
form another set of minerals, e.g. loelling-
ite (FeAs2), safforlite (CoAs), niccolite (NiAs),
rammelsbergite (NiAs2), arsenopyrite (FeAsS),
cobaltite (CoAsS), enargite (Cu3AsS,4), gersdorf-
fite (NiAsS) and the quaternary sulfide glaucodot
{(Co,Fe)AsS]. Elemental As is obtained on an
industrial scale by smelting FeAs; or FeAsS
at 650-700°C in the absence of air and
condensing the sublimed element: FeAsS ——>
FeS + As(g) ——> As(s). Residual As trapped in
the sulfide residues can be released by roasting
them in air and trapping the sublimed As?03 in
the flue system. The oxide can then either be used
directly for chemical products or reduced with
charcoal at 700-800° to give more As. As203 is
also obtained in large quantities as flue dust from
the smelting of Cu and Pb concentrates; because
of the huge scale of these operations (pp. 1174,
371) this represents the most important industrial
source of As. Some production figures and major
uses of As and its compounds are listed in the
Panel.

Stibnite, Sb2S3, is the most important
ore of antimony and it occurs in large
quantities in China, South Africa, Mexico,
Bolivia and Chile. Other sulfide ores include
ullmanite (NiSbS), livingstonite (HgSb,Ss),
tetrahedrite (Cu3SbS3), wolfsbergite (CuSbS2)
and jamesonite (FePb4Sb¢S 14). Indeed, complex
ores containing Pb, Cu, Ag and Hg are an
important industrial source of Sb. Small amounts
of oxide minerals formed by weathering are
also known, e.g. valentinite (Sb203), cervantite
(Sb204), and stibiconite (Sb204.H20), and
minor finds of native Sb have occasionally
been reported. Commercial ores have 5-60%
Sb, and recovery methods depend on the
